shared.subject: Mathematics
shared.standard_text: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions referring to the same whole and having like denominators, e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem.

t1.teacher: Core task — watch for adding denominators (3/8 + 2/8 = 5/16).
t1.student: Maria's family ate 3/8 of a veggie pizza and 2/8 of the same pizza later. How much of the pizza did they eat in all?

t2.teacher: Stretch task — subtraction from the combined amount; expect tape-diagram or equation strategies.
t2.student: After eating, 1/8 of the pizza fell on the floor. How much pizza is left to share?

exit_ticket.teacher: Sort responses: *Got it* — correct with a convincing picture or equation; *Almost there* — right answer, shaky justification; *Needs re-teaching* — added denominators.
exit_ticket.student: Jon says 4/6 + 1/6 = 5/12. Is he right? Use a picture or equation to prove your answer.

rows: The bottom number of a fraction — how many equal parts the whole is split into.
Fractions whose wholes are split into the same number of parts.

sentence_frames.label: Finish these sentences
sentence_frames.text:
I know ____________ and ____________. The part I don't know is ____________.
My strategy was ____________ because ____________.

rows: Adds denominators as well as numerators (3/8 + 2/8 = 5/16)
Treats numerator and denominator as separate whole numbers
Return to the tape diagram: how many eighths in all?

shared.anchor_activity: Finished early? Choose one problem from this page and prove your answer a second way, using a tape diagram. Then write one new question about the pizza whose answer is more than one whole, and solve it.
shared.reflect_prompt: What strategy did you use and why?

documents.title: Adding Fractions with Like Denominators — Differentiation Plan
documents.meta: 4.NF.B.3.d · 60 minutes · Below (Group A) / At (Group B) / Above (Group C)

blocks.text: All three tiers work the same pizza context with the same core problems. Only the supports differ.

blocks.label: Basis
blocks.text: Default profile applied — no diagnostic data available. If you have a recent fractions exit ticket or work sample, place students from that instead.

blocks.label: Revisable
blocks.text: Tier placement holds for this lesson only. Regroup on today's exit ticket: a Below student who solves Problem 2 unaided moves up; anyone who adds the denominators works with the Below supports tomorrow.

blocks.label: Grounded in prerequisite
blocks.text: 3.NF.A.1 — Understand a fraction 1/b as the quantity formed by 1 part when a whole is partitioned into b equal parts.

blocks.label: Worksheet tasks
blocks.text: P1 (shaded-diagram entry task + know/need table), P2, exit ticket, 'If you finish early' anchor task, Reflect prompt.

blocks.label: Worksheet tasks
blocks.text: P1, P2, exit ticket, 'If you finish early' anchor task, Reflect prompt.

blocks.label: Worksheet tasks
blocks.text: P1, P2, 'Go further' extension (write-your-own problem + error-explanation problem), exit ticket, 'If you finish early' anchor task, Reflect prompt.

The Below tier keeps both grade-level problems and adds a shaded-diagram entry and a know/need table before Problem 1 only. Students act on the model before the symbols, so the standard's full demand stays while the entry cost drops — and the scaffold fades by Problem 2.

The Above extension asks students to write and critique problems rather than compute more of them. Authoring a sum-greater-than-one story and explaining a wrong answer deepen 4.NF.B.3.d instead of moving past it, and both reuse the shared pizza context so tiers stay comparable.
Collect the exit ticket from every tier and sort into three piles: correct with a matching model; adds the denominators; miscounts the shaded parts.

blocks.label: Next day
blocks.text: Denominator pile re-enters through the shaded diagram; miscount pile recounts a partitioned model with you; correct pile starts on the anchor task.

blocks.label: Deployment
blocks.text: Printed on all three worksheets. Deploy after the exit ticket; standard-aligned because proving with a tape diagram and generating a sum-greater-than-one problem both exercise 4.NF.B.3.d.

blocks.label: For Problem 1 — try it with the diagram
blocks.text: The pizza below is cut into 8 equal slices. Shade the slices Maria's family ate first, then shade the slices they ate later in a different way.

Write your own fraction story problem where the answer is 7/8. Then write a second one where someone could make the mistake of adding the denominators — and explain how you would help them see the error.
